profile.occupation: Expert Software Engineer at Misys Banking Systems
Low Level Rust Developer at Flarion

posts.text:
Out of nearly 30,000 voters, a whopping 79% of working professionals say remote work is the perfect setting for deep, focused work. So, why are some companies still hung up on the office? 🤔

#DeepWork #RemoteWork

comments.text: Great perspective!

Remote work significantly enhances deep, focused work. Studies and surveys, including one by Crossover, show that remote workers experience fewer daily interruptions and allocate a higher percentage of their week to focus time compared to in-office workers. This environment fosters productivity and engagement

I love working remote.  Then again, I love work that I can do remotely.  I also love my workmates and management, whether live or remote.  If you are a worker, you will be most productive with whatever work environment best suits you at any particular time.  If you are a manager, you will be most productive when you manage the full spectrum from remote to onsite.  Do what works.  Don't do what doesn't.  :-)

author.occupation: Senior Software Engineer: Linux kernel, u-boot, Networking, Routing, RFID, PHP, CSS, AWS cloud, Siemens LOGO PLC

comments.text: It's impressive to see the overwhelming support for remote work among professionals. Your insights on deep, focused work are truly valuable.

Erm... Because not all work is deep, focused work? The answer is in the question. 😊

Does this survey ask about preferences for collaborative work? How many people think a full day (or multi-days) ideation workshop/training/Hackathon/ sales kickoff / strategy discussion / group creative session /etc is more productive remotely?

Don't get me wrong: I've been working remotely for over 15 years, way before it was "fashionable". I love it and wouldn't wish an 100% office-based employment to anyone. But F2F interactions is absolutely much more productive in many situations. True human interaction is also critical for team bonding, to create rapport with clients, etc...

author.occupation: AI Transformation Leader | ‘Translator’ between Tech and Business

comments.text: I agree! I have worked many jobs onsite,but switched to working remotely after having a baby! Giving my opinion here as a Mom working remotely is the best!!🤩

comments.text: Busy doesn't always mean productive! 79% say remote work boosts focus, plus it frees up commute time for actual work. Win-win! Just remember a dedicated workspace keeps the line between work & life healthy.

comments.text:
While I've been working from home almost exclusively for the past thirteen years, I truly believe in the value of occasional in-person meetings. There have been colleagues I've collaborated with for years who I finally met at an offsite event. These face-to-face interactions subtly but positively impacted our remote working relationship.

On a lighter note, it's always a bit funny how meeting someone in person after video calls for so long can reset our assumptions about their height, build, and so on!
Interesting view

article.title: Software developers don’t need performance reviews

posts.text:
My favorite quote from the article:
"Moreover, software development is commonly called a “team sport.” Assessing individual contributions in isolation can breed unhealthy competition, undermine teamwork, and incentivize behavior that, while technically hitting the mark, can be detrimental to good coding and good software. The pressure of performance evaluations can deter developers from innovative pursuits, pushing them towards safer paths."

posts.authorHeadline: Low Level Rust Developer at Flarion

posts.text:
Found this LinkedIn Insight about "career athleticism" and looked at my own path.
The first thing I'm struggling with is: where does the investment in one's learning lie? At many points in my career I noticed that I wanted to do more, learned in my private time, got more pay, but the skills I got weren't needed, so I had to change my workplace to apply the new skillset. I would've preferred to just stick to the same workplace and develop myself there. There's an old expression that says something along the lines of: "Everyone has knowledge someone else paid for." The portfolio approach makes an assumption that all skills can be acquired outside of a job, which is false. Also if a higher investment in building the portfolio is made, it will be factored into the asked salary. Another thing means that the gig itself would make its way into the employee's portfolio, would any company accept these details to be public?
The second challenge is building teams. Working in software development with gig programmers sounds a lot like consultancy, and most of the time the code quality and maintainability suffers.
Anyway that's my view on the data based candidate search.

profile.occupation: Senior Linux C++ Engineer at Riverbed Technology

posts.text: Salutare, varul meu a decis sa faca o schimbare profesionala din domeniul constructiilor in cel IT. A urmat un curs de aproximativ 1 an la Software Development Academy in urma caruia a invatat concepte pentru design web in Java si TypeScript, acum ii place sa se joace cu Kotlin pentru a scrie aplicatii pentru Android, incearca sa se obisnuiasca cu framework-ul si concepte de UI pentru usability. Nu este pretentios, e harnic, descurcaret si foarte motivat. Este vorbitor de limba Engleza si Franceza, cea din urma a practicat-o in cadrul unui proiect de constructii in strainatate. Vrea un start. Care ar fi cel mai bun punct de pornire?

comments.text: Poate sa inceapa ceva freelance projects ca backup in caz ca nu gaseste un internship

resharedPost.text:
Hello Linkedin!
I've just launched my first software engineering book, and I wanted to share this great news to all of you!
(And also give you the chance to win a free copy!)

The book is called "Shortcut to seniority", and it's available on Amazon right now (link in the comments).

I've put a lot of effort into it, over the last two years, and I wholeheartedly recommend it to anyone that wants to unlock their true potential.

I know that the pandemic has affected us all, especially those at the start of their careers, that struggle to find a new job due to Covid. 
Therefore, I would like to give a few physical copies for free to those people that will benefit from it the most, such as highschool/college students and juniors, so if you want a chance to win a free copy, please leave a comment on this post.

It would be insanely helpful if you could like and/or share this post, so it will help me reach out to as many people as possible.

Thank you all, so much!

#softwareengineering #softwaredevelopment #computersoftware

article.title: Computer Programming Skills for 2020: Survey Results from 116,000 Developers and Hiring Managers

posts.text:
TL;DR
Education paradigm change:
 - bootcamps make good hires even if they're not from a formal education background
 - best hires are people with formal education backgrounds who take up bootcamps to learn a specific skill
 - in this day and age if you want to learn something all you have to do is sit down and learn, bootcamps make that easier
 - javascript and python are highly desirable, though you can still make a good living off of C++ in Europe  & Asia
